input.text:
Abstract: This paper examines the relationship between urban green space access and reported wellbeing in post-industrial cities. Drawing on survey data from 2,400 residents across six cities, we find that proximity to maintained green space is significantly associated with higher self-reported life satisfaction (beta=0.23, p<0.001), even after controlling for income, employment status, and housing quality.

1. Introduction

Access to urban green space has long been considered a determinant of public health (WHO, 2016). However, the relationship between green space and subjective wellbeing in post-industrial contexts — where green space often occupies former industrial land with complex contamination histories — has received limited scholarly attention (Barnes & Osei, 2023; Lindström, 2024).

This paper addresses that gap by analysing original survey data collected between March and October 2025.

input.text: The new cafe opened on a Tuesday. Within a week it had a queue out the door. The owner, Maria, had spent six months converting the space from an old laundrette. 'I wanted somewhere people could just sit,' she said. 'No pressure to buy. Just sit.'
input.target_format: Web article with subheadings and a word count header

input.text:
## Introduction

The European Union's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which entered its transitional phase in October 2023, represents the first attempt by a major economic bloc to impose a carbon price on imported goods. This article examines the mechanism's design, its impact on trade relationships with developing countries, and the legal questions raised under World Trade Organization rules.

The CBAM applies initially to six sectors: cement, iron and steel, aluminium, fertilisers, electricity, and hydrogen. Importers must purchase certificates corresponding to the carbon price that would have applied had the goods been produced under the EU Emissions Trading System.

### Legal Framework

The WTO compatibility of CBAM has been challenged by several developing countries, most notably India and South Africa, which argue it constitutes a discriminatory trade barrier under GATT Article III.
